title: George III

infobox.value: (1738-06-04)4 June 1738 [[NS](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates")][[c]](#cite_note-date-3) [Norfolk House](/wiki/Norfolk_House "Norfolk House"), London, England
16 February 1820  Royal Vault, [St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle](/wiki/St_George%27s_Chapel,_Windsor_Castle "St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle")
[Frederick, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Frederick,_Prince_of_Wales "Frederick, Prince of Wales")
[Handwritten "George" with a huge leading "G" and a large capital "R" at the end for "Rex"](/wiki/File:George_III_Signature.svg "George III's signature")

(George William Frederick; 4 June 1738 – 29 January 1820) was [King of Great Britain](/wiki/King_of_Great_Britain "King of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland") from 25 October 1760 until his death in 1820. The [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") unified [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") into the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), with George as its king. He was concurrently duke and [prince-elector](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") of [Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") before becoming [King of Hanover](/wiki/King_of_Hanover "King of Hanover") on 12 October 1814. He was the first monarch of the [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") who was born in Great Britain, spoke English as his first language,[[2]](#cite_note-rh-5) and never visited Hanover.[[3]](#cite_note-6)

**George III** (George William Frederick; 4 June 1738 – 29 January 1820) was [King of Great Britain](/wiki/King_of_Great_Britain "King of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/King_of_Ireland "King of Ireland") from 25 October 1760 until his death in 1820. The [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") unified [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") into the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"), with George as its king. He was concurrently duke and [prince-elector](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") of [Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") before becoming [King of Hanover](/wiki/King_of_Hanover "King of Hanover") on 12 October 1814. He was the first monarch of the [House of Hanover](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") who was born in Great Britain, spoke English as his first language,[[2]](#cite_note-rh-5) and never visited Hanover.[[3]](#cite_note-6)

George was born during the reign of his paternal grandfather, King [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain"), as the first son of [Frederick, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Frederick,_Prince_of_Wales "Frederick, Prince of Wales"), and [Princess Augusta of Saxe-Gotha](/wiki/Princess_Augusta_of_Saxe-Gotha "Princess Augusta of Saxe-Gotha"). Following his father's death in 1751, Prince George became [heir apparent](/wiki/Heir_apparent "Heir apparent") and [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales"). He succeeded to the throne on George II's death in 1760. The following year, he married [Princess Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Princess_Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Princess Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz"), with whom he had 15 children. George III's life and reign were marked by a series of military conflicts involving his kingdoms, much of the rest of Europe, and places farther afield in Africa, the Americas and Asia. Early in his reign, Great Britain defeated [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), becoming the dominant European power in North America and India. However, Britain lost [13 of its North American colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") in the [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_War_of_Independence "American War of Independence"). Further wars against [revolutionary](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and [Napoleonic France](/wiki/Napoleonic_France "Napoleonic France") from 1793 concluded in the defeat of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") in 1815. In 1807, the [transatlantic slave trade](/wiki/Transatlantic_slave_trade "Transatlantic slave trade") was banned from the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").

In the later part of his life, George had recurrent and eventually permanent [mental illness](/wiki/Mental_illness "Mental illness"). The exact nature of George's illness is not known definitively, but historians and medical experts have suggested that his symptoms and behaviour traits were consistent with either [bipolar disorder](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder") or [porphyria](/wiki/Porphyria "Porphyria"). In 1810, George suffered a final relapse, and his eldest son, [George, Prince of Wales](/wiki/George_IV "George IV"), was named [Prince Regent](/wiki/Prince_Regent "Prince Regent") the following year. The King died aged 81, at which time the Regent succeeded him as George IV. George III reigned during much of the [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_era "Georgian era") and [Regency](/wiki/Regency_era "Regency era") eras. At the time of his death, he was the [longest-lived](/wiki/List_of_longest-living_members_of_the_British_royal_family "List of longest-living members of the British royal family") and [longest-reigning British monarch](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_in_Britain_by_length_of_reign "List of monarchs in Britain by length of reign"), having reigned for 59 years and 96 days; he remains the longest-lived and longest-reigning male monarch in British history.

## Early life

[![Conversation piece in oils: Ayscough dressed in black with a clerical collar stands beside a settee on which the two boys sit, one wearing a grey suit the other a blue one. He holds a sheet of paper; the boys hold a book.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Francis_Ayscough_with_the_Prince_of_Wales_%28later_King_George_III%29_and_Edward_Augustus%2C_Duke_of_York_and_Albany_by_Richard_Wilson.jpg/250px-Francis_Ayscough_with_the_Prince_of_Wales_%28later_King_George_III%29_and_Edward_Augustus%2C_Duke_of_York_and_Albany_by_Richard_Wilson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francis_Ayscough_with_the_Prince_of_Wales_(later_King_George_III)_and_Edward_Augustus,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany_by_Richard_Wilson.jpg)

George (right), his brother [Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Prince Edward, Duke of York and Albany"), and their tutor, [Francis Ayscough](/wiki/Francis_Ayscough "Francis Ayscough") (later [Dean](/wiki/Dean_(Christianity) "Dean (Christianity)") of [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol_Cathedral "Bristol Cathedral")), by [Richard Wilson](/wiki/Richard_Wilson_(painter) "Richard Wilson (painter)"), c. 1749

George was born on 4 June 1738 at [Norfolk House](/wiki/Norfolk_House "Norfolk House") in [St James's Square](/wiki/St_James%27s_Square "St James's Square"), London.[[c]](#cite_note-date-3) He was a grandson of King [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain") and the eldest son of [Frederick, Prince of Wales](/wiki/Frederick,_Prince_of_Wales "Frederick, Prince of Wales"), and [Augusta of Saxe-Gotha](/wiki/Augusta_of_Saxe-Gotha "Augusta of Saxe-Gotha"). As he was born two months prematurely, and thought unlikely to survive, he was baptised the same day by [Thomas Secker](/wiki/Thomas_Secker "Thomas Secker"), who was both Rector of [St James's Church, Piccadilly](/wiki/St_James%27s_Church,_Piccadilly "St James's Church, Piccadilly"), and [Bishop of Oxford](/wiki/Bishop_of_Oxford "Bishop of Oxford").[[4]](#cite_note-7)[[5]](#cite_note-8) One month later, he was publicly baptised at Norfolk House, again by Secker. His godparents were King [Frederick I of Sweden](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Sweden "Frederick I of Sweden") (for whom [Lord Baltimore](/wiki/Charles_Calvert,_5th_Baron_Baltimore "Charles Calvert, 5th Baron Baltimore") stood proxy), his uncle [Frederick III, Duke of Saxe-Gotha](/wiki/Frederick_III,_Duke_of_Saxe-Gotha "Frederick III, Duke of Saxe-Gotha") (for whom [Lord Carnarvon](/wiki/Henry_Brydges,_2nd_Duke_of_Chandos "Henry Brydges, 2nd Duke of Chandos") stood proxy), and his great-aunt [Sophia Dorothea, Queen in Prussia](/wiki/Sophia_Dorothea,_Queen_in_Prussia "Sophia Dorothea, Queen in Prussia") (for whom [Lady Charlotte Edwin](/wiki/James_Hamilton,_4th_Duke_of_Hamilton#Marriage_and_issue "James Hamilton, 4th Duke of Hamilton") stood proxy).[[6]](#cite_note-9)

George grew into a healthy, reserved and shy child. The family moved to [Leicester Square](/wiki/Leicester_Square "Leicester Square"), where George and his younger brother [Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Prince Edward, Duke of York and Albany") (later [Duke of York and Albany](/wiki/Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Duke of York and Albany")) were educated together by private tutors. Family letters show that he could read and write in both English and German, as well as comment on political events of the time, by the age of eight.[[7]](#cite_note-10) He was the first [British monarch](/wiki/British_monarch "British monarch") to study science systematically.[[8]](#cite_note-Brooke,_pp._42–44,_55-11)

Apart from chemistry and physics, his lessons included astronomy, mathematics, French, Latin, history, music, geography, commerce, agriculture and constitutional law, along with sporting and social accomplishments such as dancing, fencing and riding. His religious education was wholly [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican").[[8]](#cite_note-Brooke,_pp._42–44,_55-11) At the age of 10, George took part in a family production of [Joseph Addison](/wiki/Joseph_Addison "Joseph Addison")'s play *[Cato](/wiki/Cato,_a_Tragedy "Cato, a Tragedy")* and said in the new prologue: "What, tho' a boy! It may with truth be said, A boy in *England* born, in England bred."[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12) Historian [Romney Sedgwick](/wiki/Romney_Sedgwick "Romney Sedgwick") argued that these lines appear "to be the source of the only historical phrase with which he is associated".[[10]](#cite_note-13)

King George II disliked Prince Frederick and took little interest in his grandchildren. However, in 1751, Frederick died unexpectedly from a lung injury at the age of 44, and his son George became [heir apparent](/wiki/Heir_apparent "Heir apparent") to the throne and inherited his father's title of [Duke of Edinburgh](/wiki/Duke_of_Edinburgh "Duke of Edinburgh"). The King now took more interest in his grandson and created him [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") three weeks later.[[11]](#cite_note-14)[[12]](#cite_note-15)

[![Head-and-shoulders portrait of a young clean-shaven George wearing a finely-embroidered jacket, the blue sash of the Order of the Garter, and a powdered wig.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/George%2C_Prince_of_Wales_%281738-1820%29%2C_by_Jean-%C3%89tienne_Liotard.jpg/250px-George%2C_Prince_of_Wales_%281738-1820%29%2C_by_Jean-%C3%89tienne_Liotard.jpg)](/wiki/File:George,_Prince_of_Wales_(1738-1820),_by_Jean-%C3%89tienne_Liotard.jpg)

Pastel of George as [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") by [Jean-Étienne Liotard](/wiki/Jean-%C3%89tienne_Liotard "Jean-Étienne Liotard"), 1754

In the spring of 1756, as George approached his eighteenth birthday, the King offered him a grand establishment at [St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace"), but George refused the offer, guided by his mother and her confidant, [John Stuart, 3rd Earl of Bute](/wiki/John_Stuart,_3rd_Earl_of_Bute "John Stuart, 3rd Earl of Bute"), who later served as [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom").[[13]](#cite_note-16) George's mother, now the [Dowager](/wiki/Dowager "Dowager") Princess of Wales, preferred to keep George at home where she could imbue him with her strict moral values.[[14]](#cite_note-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18)

## Accession and marriage

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Sir_Joshua_Reynolds_%281723-92%29_-_The_Marriage_of_George_III_-_RCIN_404353_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-Sir_Joshua_Reynolds_%281723-92%29_-_The_Marriage_of_George_III_-_RCIN_404353_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Joshua_Reynolds_(1723-92)_-_The_Marriage_of_George_III_-_RCIN_404353_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

Wedding of George and Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz, oil sketch by Sir [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds"), c. 1761

In 1759, George was smitten with [Lady Sarah Lennox](/wiki/Lady_Sarah_Lennox "Lady Sarah Lennox"), sister of [Charles Lennox, 3rd Duke of Richmond](/wiki/Charles_Lennox,_3rd_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Lennox, 3rd Duke of Richmond"), but Lord Bute advised against the match and George abandoned his thoughts of marriage. "I am born for the happiness or misery of a great nation," he wrote, "and consequently must often act contrary to my passions."[[16]](#cite_note-19) Nevertheless, George and his mother resisted attempts by the King to marry George to [Princess Sophie Caroline of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel](/wiki/Princess_Sophie_Caroline_of_Brunswick-Wolfenb%C3%BCttel "Princess Sophie Caroline of Brunswick-Wolfenbüttel").[[17]](#cite_note-20)

The following year, at the age of 22, George succeeded to the throne when his grandfather George II died suddenly on 25 October 1760, at age 76. The search for a suitable wife intensified: after giving consideration to a number of [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") German princesses, George's mother sent Colonel [David Graeme](/wiki/David_Graeme_(British_Army_officer) "David Graeme (British Army officer)") with, on her son's behalf, an offer of marriage to [Princess Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Princess_Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Princess Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz"). Charlotte accepted. While a [royal household](/wiki/Household#History "Household") and staff were assembled for Charlotte in London, [Lord Harcourt](/wiki/Simon_Harcourt,_1st_Earl_Harcourt "Simon Harcourt, 1st Earl Harcourt"), the royal [Master of the Horse](/wiki/Master_of_the_Horse_(United_Kingdom) "Master of the Horse (United Kingdom)"), escorted her from [Neustrelitz](/wiki/Neustrelitz "Neustrelitz") to London. Charlotte arrived in the afternoon of 8 September 1761 and the marriage ceremony was conducted that same evening in the [Chapel Royal](/wiki/Chapel_Royal "Chapel Royal"), St James's Palace.[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[d]](#cite_note-23) [George and Charlotte's coronation](/wiki/George_and_Charlotte%27s_coronation "George and Charlotte's coronation") was held at [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey") a fortnight later on 22 September. George never took a mistress (in contrast with his father, his grandfather, and his sons), and the couple enjoyed a happy marriage until his mental illness struck.[[2]](#cite_note-rh-5)[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12)

[![Imaginary garden scene with birds of paradise, vines laden with grapes, and architectural columns. The two young princesses and their baby sister wear fine dresses and play with three spaniels and a tambourine.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Daughters_of_King_George_III.jpg/250px-Daughters_of_King_George_III.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daughters_of_King_George_III.jpg)

Three of George's daughters, [Mary](/wiki/Princess_Mary,_Duchess_of_Gloucester_and_Edinburgh "Princess Mary, Duchess of Gloucester and Edinburgh") (left), [Sophia](/wiki/Princess_Sophia_of_the_United_Kingdom "Princess Sophia of the United Kingdom") (right), and [Amelia](/wiki/Princess_Amelia_of_the_United_Kingdom "Princess Amelia of the United Kingdom") (baby), painted by [John Singleton Copley](/wiki/John_Singleton_Copley "John Singleton Copley"), c. 1785

The King and Queen had 15 children—nine sons and six daughters. In 1762, George purchased Buckingham House (on the site now occupied by [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace")) for use as a family retreat.[[20]](#cite_note-24) His other residences were [Kew Palace](/wiki/Kew_Palace "Kew Palace") and [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle"). St James's Palace was retained for official use. He did not travel extensively and spent his entire life in southern England. In the 1790s, the King and his family took holidays at [Weymouth, Dorset](/wiki/Weymouth,_Dorset "Weymouth, Dorset"),[[21]](#cite_note-25) which he thus popularised as one of the first seaside resorts in England.[[22]](#cite_note-26)

## Early reign

### Early regnal years

Further information: [Great Britain in the Seven Years' War](/wiki/Great_Britain_in_the_Seven_Years%27_War "Great Britain in the Seven Years' War")

George, in his accession speech to [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), proclaimed: "Born and educated in this country, I glory in the name of Britain."[[23]](#cite_note-27) He inserted this phrase into the speech, written by [Lord Hardwicke](/wiki/Lord_Hardwicke "Lord Hardwicke"), to demonstrate his desire to distance himself from his German forebears, who were perceived as caring more for Hanover than for Britain.[[24]](#cite_note-28) During George III's lengthy reign, Britain was a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), ruled by his ministerial government and prominent men in Parliament.[[25]](#cite_note-29) Although his accession was at first welcomed by politicians of all parties,[[e]](#cite_note-31) the first years of his reign were marked by political instability, largely as a result of disagreements over the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War").[[27]](#cite_note-32) George came to be perceived as favouring [Tory](/wiki/Tories_(British_political_party) "Tories (British political party)") ministers, which led to his denunciation by the [Whigs](/wiki/Whigs_(British_political_party) "Whigs (British political party)") as an [autocrat](/wiki/Autocrat "Autocrat").[[2]](#cite_note-rh-5)

On his accession, the [Crown lands](/wiki/Crown_land "Crown land") produced relatively little income; most revenue was generated through taxes and excise duties. George surrendered the [Crown Estate](/wiki/Crown_Estate "Crown Estate") to Parliamentary control in return for a [civil list](/wiki/Civil_list "Civil list") annuity for the support of his household and the expenses of civil government.[[28]](#cite_note-33) Claims that he used the income to reward supporters with bribes and gifts[[29]](#cite_note-34) are disputed by historians who say such claims "rest on nothing but falsehoods put out by disgruntled opposition".[[30]](#cite_note-35) Debts amounting to over £3 million over the course of George's reign were paid by Parliament, and the civil list annuity was increased from time to time.[[31]](#cite_note-36) He aided the [Royal Academy of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts") with large grants from his private funds,[[32]](#cite_note-37) and may have donated more than half of his personal income to charity.[[33]](#cite_note-38) Of his art collection, the two most notable purchases are [Johannes Vermeer](/wiki/Johannes_Vermeer "Johannes Vermeer")'s *[Lady at the Virginals](/wiki/The_Music_Lesson "The Music Lesson")* and a set of [Canalettos](/wiki/Canaletto "Canaletto"), but it is as a collector of books that he is best remembered.[[34]](#cite_note-39) The [King's Library](/wiki/King%27s_Library "King's Library") was open and available to scholars and was the foundation of a new national library.[[35]](#cite_note-40)

### Legislation and politics

[![Quarter-length portrait in oils of a clean-shaven young George in profile wearing a red suit, the Garter star, a blue sash, and a powdered wig. He has a receding chin and his forehead slopes away from the bridge of his nose making his head look round in shape.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/George_III_%28by_Allan_Ramsay%29.jpg/250px-George_III_%28by_Allan_Ramsay%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_III_(by_Allan_Ramsay).jpg)

Portrait by [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(artist) "Allan Ramsay (artist)"), 1762

In May 1762, the incumbent Whig government of [Thomas Pelham-Holles, 1st Duke of Newcastle](/wiki/Thomas_Pelham-Holles,_1st_Duke_of_Newcastle "Thomas Pelham-Holles, 1st Duke of Newcastle"), was replaced with one led by Lord Bute, a Scottish Tory. Bute's opponents worked against him by spreading the calumny that he was having an affair with the King's mother, and by exploiting [anti-Scottish sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Scottish_sentiment "Anti-Scottish sentiment") amongst the English.[[36]](#cite_note-41) [John Wilkes](/wiki/John_Wilkes "John Wilkes"), a member of parliament, published *[The North Briton](/wiki/The_North_Briton "The North Briton")*, which was both inflammatory and defamatory in its condemnation of Bute and the government. Wilkes was eventually arrested for [seditious libel](/wiki/Seditious_libel "Seditious libel") but he fled to France to escape punishment; he was expelled from the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain") and found guilty *[in absentia](/wiki/Trial_in_absentia "Trial in absentia")* of blasphemy and libel.[[37]](#cite_note-42) In 1763, after concluding the [Peace of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") which ended the war, Lord Bute resigned, allowing the Whigs under [George Grenville](/wiki/George_Grenville "George Grenville") to return to power. Britain received enormous concessions, including [West Florida](/wiki/West_Florida "West Florida"). Britain restored to France lucrative slave-sugar islands in the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies"), including [Guadeloupe](/wiki/Guadeloupe "Guadeloupe") and [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique"). The treaty also ended French control over nearly all of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"); [Canada](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)") was ceded to Britain in its entirety, while [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana_(New_France) "Louisiana (New France)") was divided in two, with the eastern half (from the [Allegheny Mountains](/wiki/Allegheny_Mountains "Allegheny Mountains") to the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River")) ceded to Britain and the western half, plus the city of [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), ceded to Spain.[[38]](#cite_note-43)

Later that year, the [Royal Proclamation of 1763](/wiki/Royal_Proclamation_of_1763 "Royal Proclamation of 1763") placed a limit upon the westward expansion of the American colonies and created an [Indian reserve](/wiki/Indian_Reserve_(1763) "Indian Reserve (1763)"). The Proclamation aimed to divert colonial expansion to the north (to [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia")) and to the south (Florida), and protect the British fur trade with the Indians.[[39]](#cite_note-44) The Proclamation Line did not bother the majority of settled farmers, but it was unpopular with a vocal minority. This discontent ultimately contributed to conflict between the colonists and the British government.[[40]](#cite_note-45) With the American colonists generally unburdened by British taxes, the government thought it appropriate for them to pay towards the defence of the colonies against native uprisings and the possibility of French incursions.[[f]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/George_III%27s_Procession_to_the_Houses_of_Parliament.jpg/250px-George_III%27s_Procession_to_the_Houses_of_Parliament.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_III%27s_Procession_to_the_Houses_of_Parliament.jpg)

George travelling to the [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") in the [Gold State Coach](/wiki/Gold_State_Coach "Gold State Coach"), 1762

The central issue for the colonists was not the amount of taxes but whether Parliament could levy a tax without American approval, for there were no American seats in Parliament.[[43]](#cite_note-49) The Americans protested that like all Englishmen they had rights to "[no taxation without representation](/wiki/No_taxation_without_representation "No taxation without representation")". In 1765, Grenville introduced the [Stamp Act](/wiki/Stamp_Act_1765 "Stamp Act 1765"), which levied a [stamp duty](/wiki/Stamp_duty "Stamp duty") on every document in the British colonies in North America. Since newspapers were printed on stamped paper, those most affected by the introduction of the duty were the most effective at producing propaganda opposing the tax.[[44]](#cite_note-50)

Meanwhile, George had become exasperated at Grenville's attempts to reduce the King's prerogatives, and tried, unsuccessfully, to persuade [William Pitt the Elder](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Elder "William Pitt the Elder") to accept the office of prime minister.[[45]](#cite_note-51) After a brief illness, which may have presaged his illnesses to come, George settled on [Charles Watson-Wentworth, 2nd Marquess of Rockingham](/wiki/Charles_Watson-Wentworth,_2nd_Marquess_of_Rockingham "Charles Watson-Wentworth, 2nd Marquess of Rockingham") to form a ministry, and dismissed Grenville.[[46]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/King_George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom%2C_by_John_van_Nost_the_Younger_1767_CE._It_is_housed_in_the_British_Museum%2C_London%3B_lent_by_the_Victoria_and_Albert_Museum.JPG/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom,_by_John_van_Nost_the_Younger_1767_CE._It_is_housed_in_the_British_Museum,_London;_lent_by_the_Victoria_and_Albert_Museum.JPG)

Bust by [John van Nost the younger](/wiki/John_van_Nost_the_younger "John van Nost the younger"), 1767

Lord Rockingham, with the support of Pitt and the King, repealed Grenville's unpopular Stamp Act. Rockingham's government was weak, and he was replaced as prime minister in 1766 by Pitt, whom George created [Earl of Chatham](/wiki/Earl_of_Chatham "Earl of Chatham"). The actions of Lord Chatham and George III in repealing the Act were so popular in America that statues of them both were erected in [New York City](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1665%E2%80%931783) "History of New York City (1665–1783)").[[47]](#cite_note-53) Lord Chatham fell ill in 1767, and [Augustus FitzRoy, 3rd Duke of Grafton](/wiki/Augustus_FitzRoy,_3rd_Duke_of_Grafton "Augustus FitzRoy, 3rd Duke of Grafton"), took over the government. Grafton did not formally become prime minister until 1768. That year, John Wilkes returned to England, stood as a candidate in the [general election](/wiki/1768_British_general_election "1768 British general election"), and came top of the poll in the [Middlesex constituency](/wiki/Middlesex_constituency "Middlesex constituency"). Wilkes was again expelled from Parliament. He was re-elected and expelled twice more, before the House of Commons resolved that his candidature was invalid and declared [the runner-up](/wiki/Henry_Luttrell,_2nd_Earl_of_Carhampton "Henry Luttrell, 2nd Earl of Carhampton") as the victor.[[48]](#cite_note-54) Grafton's government disintegrated in 1770, allowing the Tories led by [Lord North](/wiki/Lord_North "Lord North") to return to power.[[49]](#cite_note-55)

### Family issues

George was deeply devout and spent hours in prayer,[[50]](#cite_note-56) but his piety was not shared by his brothers. George was appalled by what he saw as their loose morals. In 1770, his brother [Prince Henry, Duke of Cumberland and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Henry,_Duke_of_Cumberland_and_Strathearn "Prince Henry, Duke of Cumberland and Strathearn"), was exposed as an adulterer. The following year, Henry married a young widow, [Anne Horton](/wiki/Anne_Horton "Anne Horton"). The King considered her inappropriate as a royal bride: she was from a lower social class and German law barred any children of the couple from the Hanoverian succession.[[51]](#cite_note-Brooke_pp._272-57)

George insisted on a new law that essentially forbade members of the royal family from legally marrying without the consent of the sovereign. The subsequent bill was unpopular in Parliament, including among George's own ministers, but passed as the [Royal Marriages Act 1772](/wiki/Royal_Marriages_Act_1772 "Royal Marriages Act 1772"). Shortly afterwards, another of George's brothers, [Prince William Henry, Duke of Gloucester and Edinburgh](/wiki/Prince_William_Henry,_Duke_of_Gloucester_and_Edinburgh "Prince William Henry, Duke of Gloucester and Edinburgh"), revealed he had been secretly married to [Maria, Countess Waldegrave](/wiki/Maria,_Duchess_of_Gloucester_and_Edinburgh "Maria, Duchess of Gloucester and Edinburgh"), the illegitimate daughter of Sir [Edward Walpole](/wiki/Edward_Walpole "Edward Walpole"). The news confirmed George's opinion that he had been right to introduce the law: Maria was related to his political opponents. Neither lady was ever received at court.[[51]](#cite_note-Brooke_pp._272-57)

### Increasing issues in America

[![Three-quarter length seated portrait of a clean-shaven George with a fleshy face and white eyebrows wearing a powdered wig.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/George-III-by-Johan-Joseph-Zoffany.jpg/250px-George-III-by-Johan-Joseph-Zoffany.jpg)](/wiki/File:George-III-by-Johan-Joseph-Zoffany.jpg)

[Portrait of George](/wiki/Portrait_of_George_III_(Zoffany) "Portrait of George III (Zoffany)") in army uniform and the [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter"), by [Johan Zoffany](/wiki/Johan_Zoffany "Johan Zoffany"), 1771

Lord North's government was chiefly concerned with discontent in America. To assuage American opinion most of the custom duties were withdrawn, except for the tea duty, which in George's words was "one tax to keep up the right [to levy taxes]".[[52]](#cite_note-58) In 1773, the tea ships moored in [Boston Harbor](/wiki/Boston_Harbor "Boston Harbor") were boarded by colonists and the tea was thrown overboard, an event that became known as the [Boston Tea Party](/wiki/Boston_Tea_Party "Boston Tea Party"). In Britain, opinion hardened against the colonists, with Chatham now agreeing with North that the destruction of the tea was "certainly criminal".[[53]](#cite_note-59)

With the clear support of Parliament, Lord North introduced measures, which were called the [Intolerable Acts](/wiki/Intolerable_Acts "Intolerable Acts") by the colonists: the [Port of Boston](/wiki/Port_of_Boston "Port of Boston") was shut down and the [charter](/wiki/Explanatory_charter "Explanatory charter") of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Province_of_Massachusetts_Bay "Province of Massachusetts Bay") was [altered](/wiki/Massachusetts_Government_Act "Massachusetts Government Act") so that the upper house of [the legislature](/wiki/Massachusetts_General_Court "Massachusetts General Court") was appointed by [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") instead of elected by the lower house.[[54]](#cite_note-60) Up to this point, in the words of Professor Peter Thomas, George's "hopes were centred on a political solution, and he always bowed to his [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cabinet of the United Kingdom")'s opinions even when sceptical of their success. The detailed evidence of the years from 1763 to 1775 tends to exonerate George III from any real responsibility for the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution")."[[55]](#cite_note-61) Though both the Americans and older British historians characterised George as a tyrant, in these years he acted as a constitutional monarch supporting the initiatives of his ministers.[[56]](#cite_note-62)

## American War of Independence

Main articles: [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") and [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/William_Walcutt_statue_George_III.png/250px-William_Walcutt_statue_George_III.png)](/wiki/File:William_Walcutt_statue_George_III.png)

*Pulling Down the Statue of George III at Bowling Green, 9 July 1776*, [William Walcutt](/wiki/William_Walcutt "William Walcutt") (1854)

The [American War of Independence](/wiki/American_War_of_Independence "American War of Independence") was the culmination of the civil and political [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). Armed conflict began between British regulars and colonial militiamen at the [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord") in April 1775. The [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") sent [petitions to the Crown for intervention with Parliament](/wiki/Olive_Branch_Petition "Olive Branch Petition"), but the King and Parliament ignored them. George declared the American leaders [to be traitors](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Rebellion "Proclamation of Rebellion") and a year of fighting ensued. [Thomas Paine](/wiki/Thomas_Paine "Thomas Paine")'s book *[Common Sense](/wiki/Common_Sense "Common Sense")* referred to George III as "the Royal Brute of Great Britain".[[57]](#cite_note-63)

The colonies [declared their independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") in July 1776, listing [twenty-seven grievances](/wiki/Grievances_of_the_United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "Grievances of the United States Declaration of Independence") against the British king and legislature while asking the support of the populace. Among George's other offences, the declaration charged, "He has abdicated Government here ... He has plundered our seas, ravaged our coasts, burnt our towns, and destroyed the lives of our people." The gilded equestrian statue of the King in New York was pulled down.[[58]](#cite_note-64) The British captured the city in 1776 but lost Boston, and the [grand strategic plan](/wiki/Saratoga_campaign "Saratoga campaign") of invading from Canada and cutting off New England failed with the surrender of British Lieutenant-General [John Burgoyne](/wiki/John_Burgoyne "John Burgoyne") following the [battles of Saratoga](/wiki/Battles_of_Saratoga "Battles of Saratoga").[[59]](#cite_note-65)

Prime Minister [Lord North](/wiki/Lord_North "Lord North") was not an ideal war leader, but George III managed to give Parliament a sense of purpose to fight, and North was able to keep [his cabinet](/wiki/North_ministry "North ministry") together. North's cabinet ministers [John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich](/wiki/John_Montagu,_4th_Earl_of_Sandwich "John Montagu, 4th Earl of Sandwich"), [First Lord of the Admiralty](/wiki/First_Lord_of_the_Admiralty "First Lord of the Admiralty"), and [Lord George Germain](/wiki/Lord_George_Germain "Lord George Germain"), [Secretary of State for the Colonies](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for the Colonies"), proved to lack leadership skills suited for their positions.[[60]](#cite_note-66)

George III is often accused of obstinately trying to keep Great Britain at war with the rebels, despite the opinions of his own ministers.[[61]](#cite_note-67) In the words of British historian [George Otto Trevelyan](/wiki/George_Otto_Trevelyan "George Otto Trevelyan"), the King was determined "never to acknowledge the independence of the Americans, and to punish their contumacy by the indefinite prolongation of a war which promised to be eternal."[[62]](#cite_note-68) He wanted to "keep the rebels harassed, anxious, and poor, until the day when, by a natural and inevitable process, discontent and disappointment were converted into penitence and remorse".[[63]](#cite_note-69) Later historians defend George by saying that, in the context of the times, no king would willingly surrender such a large territory,[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12)[[64]](#cite_note-cg-70) and his conduct was far less ruthless than contemporaneous monarchs in Europe.[[65]](#cite_note-71) After Saratoga, both Parliament and the British people were in favour of the war; recruitment ran at high levels and political opponents, though vocal, remained a small minority.[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12)[[66]](#cite_note-72)

With the setbacks in America, Lord North asked to transfer power to [Lord Chatham](/wiki/Lord_Chatham "Lord Chatham"), whom he thought more capable, but George refused to do so; he suggested instead that Chatham serve as a subordinate minister in North's administration, but Chatham refused; he died later in the same year.[[67]](#cite_note-73) North was allied to the "King's Friends" in Parliament and believed that George III had the right to exercise powers.[[68]](#cite_note-74) In early 1778, [Louis XVI](/wiki/Louis_XVI "Louis XVI") of France (Britain's chief rival) signed a [treaty of alliance](/wiki/Treaty_of_Alliance_(1778) "Treaty of Alliance (1778)") with the United States.[[69]](#cite_note-Willcox161165-75) The [French Navy](/wiki/French_Navy "French Navy") outran the British naval blockade of the Mediterranean and sailed to America.[[69]](#cite_note-Willcox161165-75) The conflict now affected America, Europe, and India.[[69]](#cite_note-Willcox161165-75) [Charles III of Spain](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain") had misgivings because of his own colonies but decided to side with France in the war in limited fashion in 1779.[[70]](#cite_note-76) One faction of the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") aided the Americans, whereas another aided Britain, whose allies included [Loyalists](/wiki/Loyalists_(American_Revolution) "Loyalists (American Revolution)") and [German auxiliaries](/wiki/Germans_in_the_American_Revolution#Allies_of_Great_Britain "Germans in the American Revolution"). [Lord Gower](/wiki/Granville_Leveson-Gower,_1st_Marquess_of_Stafford "Granville Leveson-Gower, 1st Marquess of Stafford") and [Lord Weymouth](/wiki/Thomas_Thynne,_1st_Marquess_of_Bath "Thomas Thynne, 1st Marquess of Bath") both resigned from the government. Lord North again requested that he also be allowed to resign, but he stayed in office at George III's insistence.[[71]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Benjamin_West_%281738-1820%29_-_George_III_%281738-1820%29_-_RCIN_405407_-_Royal_Collection.jpg/250px-Benjamin_West_%281738-1820%29_-_George_III_%281738-1820%29_-_RCIN_405407_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benjamin_West_(1738-1820)_-_George_III_(1738-1820)_-_RCIN_405407_-_Royal_Collection.jpg)

George III in 1779, with symbols of rule, scenes of the Royal Navy and British Army at war, by [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West")

During the summer of 1779, a combined French–Spanish naval fleet threatened to invade England and transport 31,000 French troops across the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). George III said that Britain was confronted by the "most serious crisis the nation ever knew". In August, 66 warships entered the English Channel, but sickness, hunger, and adverse winds forced the French–Spanish [armada](/wiki/Armada_of_1779 "Armada of 1779") to withdraw, ending the invasion threat.[[72]](#cite_note-78)

In late 1779, George III advocated sending more British warships and troops across the Atlantic to the West Indies. He boldly said: "We must risk something, otherwise we will only vegetate in this war. I own I wish either with spirit to get through it, or with a crash be ruined." In January 1780, 7,000 British troops under General Sir [John Vaughan](/wiki/John_Vaughan_(British_Army_officer,_died_1795) "John Vaughan (British Army officer, died 1795)") were transported to the West Indies.[[73]](#cite_note-79) Nonetheless, opposition to the costly war was increasing, and in June 1780 contributed to disturbances in London known as the [Gordon riots](/wiki/Gordon_riots "Gordon riots").[[74]](#cite_note-80)

As late as the [siege of Charleston](/wiki/Siege_of_Charleston "Siege of Charleston") in 1780, Loyalists could still believe in their eventual victory, as British troops inflicted defeats on the Continental forces at the [Battle of Camden](/wiki/Battle_of_Camden "Battle of Camden") and the [Battle of Guilford Court House](/wiki/Battle_of_Guilford_Court_House "Battle of Guilford Court House").[[75]](#cite_note-81) In late 1781, the news of [Lord Cornwallis](/wiki/Lord_Cornwallis "Lord Cornwallis")'s surrender at the [siege of Yorktown](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") reached London; North's parliamentary support ebbed away and he resigned the following year. George drafted an abdication notice, which was never delivered.[[64]](#cite_note-cg-70)[[76]](#cite_note-82) He finally accepted the defeat in America and authorized peace negotiations. Britain formally recognized the independence of the United States in the [Treaties of Paris](/wiki/Peace_of_Paris_(1783) "Peace of Paris (1783)") signed in 1782 and 1783.[[77]](#cite_note-83) In early 1783, George III privately conceded "America is lost!" He reflected that the Northern colonies had developed into Britain's "successful rivals" in commercial trade and fishing.[[78]](#cite_note-84) Up to 70,000 Loyalists fled to [Canada](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") (where they became known as the [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalists "United Empire Loyalists")), the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), or England after their homes and businesses were looted and destroyed by pro-revolutionary Americans.[[79]](#cite_note-85)

[John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") was appointed American [minister to London](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_the_United_States_to_the_United_Kingdom "List of ambassadors of the United States to the United Kingdom") in 1785, by which time George had become resigned to the new relationship between his country and the former colonies. He told Adams, "I was the last to consent to the separation; but the separation having been made and having become inevitable, I have always said, as I say now, that I would be the first to meet the friendship of the United States as an independent power."[[80]](#cite_note-86)

## Mid-reign

### Government

[![Centre: George III, drawn as a paunchy man with pockets bulging with gold coins, receives a wheel-barrow filled with money-bags from William Pitt, whose pockets also overflow with coin. To the left, a quadriplegic veteran begs on the street. To the right, George, Prince of Wales, is depicted dressed in rags.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/National-Debt-Gillray.jpeg/250px-National-Debt-Gillray.jpeg)](/wiki/File:National-Debt-Gillray.jpeg)

In *A new way to pay the National Debt* (1786), [James Gillray](/wiki/James_Gillray "James Gillray") caricatured King George III and Queen Charlotte awash with treasury funds to cover royal debts, with Pitt handing him another [money bag](/wiki/Money_bag "Money bag").

With the collapse of Lord North's ministry in 1782, the Whig Lord Rockingham became prime minister for the second time, but died within months. The King then appointed [William Petty, 2nd Earl of Shelburne](/wiki/William_Petty,_2nd_Earl_of_Shelburne "William Petty, 2nd Earl of Shelburne") to replace him. [Charles James Fox](/wiki/Charles_James_Fox "Charles James Fox"), however, refused to serve under Shelburne, and demanded the appointment of [William Cavendish-Bentinck, 3rd Duke of Portland](/wiki/William_Cavendish-Bentinck,_3rd_Duke_of_Portland "William Cavendish-Bentinck, 3rd Duke of Portland"). In 1783, the House of Commons forced Shelburne from office and his government was replaced by the [Fox–North Coalition](/wiki/Fox%E2%80%93North_Coalition "Fox–North Coalition"). Portland became prime minister, with Fox and Lord North, as foreign secretary and home secretary respectively.[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12)

The King disliked Fox intensely, for his politics as well as his character: he thought Fox unprincipled and a bad influence on the Prince of Wales.[[81]](#cite_note-87) George III was distressed at having to appoint ministers not of his liking, but the Portland ministry quickly built up a majority in the House of Commons, and could not be displaced easily. He was further dismayed when the government introduced the India Bill, which proposed to reform the government of India by transferring political power from the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") to Parliamentary commissioners.[[82]](#cite_note-88) Although George actually favoured greater control over the company, the proposed commissioners were all political allies of Fox.[[83]](#cite_note-89) Immediately after the House of Commons passed it, George authorised [Lord Temple](/wiki/George_Nugent-Temple-Grenville,_1st_Marquess_of_Buckingham "George Nugent-Temple-Grenville, 1st Marquess of Buckingham") to inform the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") that he would regard any peer who voted for the bill as his enemy. The bill was rejected by the Lords; three days later, the Portland ministry was dismissed, and [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") was appointed prime minister, with Temple as his secretary of state. On 17 December 1783, the Commons voted in favour of a motion condemning the influence of the monarch in parliamentary voting as a "high crime" and Temple was forced to resign. Temple's departure destabilised the government, and three months later the government lost its majority and Parliament was dissolved; the subsequent [election](/wiki/1784_British_general_election "1784 British general election") gave Pitt a firm mandate.[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12)

Pitt's appointment was a great victory for George. It proved that the King could appoint prime ministers on the basis of his own interpretation of the public mood without having to follow the choice of the current majority in the House of Commons. Throughout Pitt's ministry, George supported many of Pitt's political aims and created new peers at an unprecedented rate to increase the number of Pitt's supporters in the House of Lords.[[84]](#cite_note-90) During and after Pitt's ministry, George was extremely popular in Britain.[[85]](#cite_note-91) The British people admired him for his piety and for remaining faithful to his wife.[[86]](#cite_note-92) He was fond of his children and was devastated at the death of two of his sons in infancy, in 1782 and 1783 respectively.[[87]](#cite_note-93) Nevertheless, he set his children a strict regimen. They were expected to attend rigorous lessons from seven in the morning and to lead lives of religious observance and virtue.[[88]](#cite_note-94) When his children strayed from George's principles of righteousness, as his sons did as young adults, he was dismayed and disappointed.[[89]](#cite_note-95)

### Illness

[![Gold coin bearing the profile of a round-headed George wearing a classical Roman-style haircut and a laurel wreath.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/GeorgeIIIGuinea.jpg/250px-GeorgeIIIGuinea.jpg)](/wiki/File:GeorgeIIIGuinea.jpg)

Gold [guinea](/wiki/Guinea_(British_coin) "Guinea (British coin)") of George III, 1789

By this time, George's health was deteriorating. He suffered from a [mental illness](/wiki/Mental_illness "Mental illness") characterised by acute [mania](/wiki/Mania "Mania"). Until the mid-20th century, the King's illness was generally considered to be psychological. In 1966, a study by [Ida Macalpine](/wiki/Ida_Macalpine "Ida Macalpine") and [Richard Hunter](/wiki/Richard_Alfred_Hunter "Richard Alfred Hunter") suggested that the illness was physiological, caused by the blood disease [porphyria](/wiki/Porphyria "Porphyria").[[90]](#cite_note-96) Although meeting with some contemporary opposition,[[91]](#cite_note-97) the view subsequently gained widespread scholarly acceptance.[[92]](#cite_note-98) A study of samples of George's hair published in 2005 revealed high levels of [arsenic](/wiki/Arsenic "Arsenic"), a cause of metabolic blood disorders and thus a possible trigger for porphyria. The source of the arsenic is not known, but it could have been a component of medicines or cosmetics.[[93]](#cite_note-99) The theory was also established in the public mind through influential dramatisations, such as [Alan Bennett](/wiki/Alan_Bennett "Alan Bennett")'s play *[The Madness of George III](/wiki/The_Madness_of_George_III "The Madness of George III")*, and in [Nicholas Hytner](/wiki/Nicholas_Hytner "Nicholas Hytner")'s subsequent [film](/wiki/The_Madness_of_King_George "The Madness of King George"). From 2010, this view has been increasingly challenged, and Macalpine and Hunter's study criticised.[[94]](#cite_note-100)[[95]](#cite_note-101)[[96]](#cite_note-102) Recent scholarship discounts the porphyria theory and contends that George's illness was psychiatric, most probably [bipolar disorder](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder").[[97]](#cite_note-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Thomas_Lawrence_%281769-1830%29_-_George_III_%281738%E2%80%931820%29_-_VA.1950.0023.0001_-_Herbert_Art_Gallery_and_Museum.jpg/250px-Thomas_Lawrence_%281769-1830%29_-_George_III_%281738%E2%80%931820%29_-_VA.1950.0023.0001_-_Herbert_Art_Gallery_and_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Lawrence_(1769-1830)_-_George_III_(1738%E2%80%931820)_-_VA.1950.0023.0001_-_Herbert_Art_Gallery_and_Museum.jpg)

Portrait by [Thomas Lawrence](/wiki/Thomas_Lawrence "Thomas Lawrence"), shown at the [Royal Academy Exhibition of 1792](/wiki/Royal_Academy_Exhibition_of_1792 "Royal Academy Exhibition of 1792")

George may have had a brief episode of disease in 1765, and a longer episode began in the summer of 1788. At the end of the parliamentary session, he went to [Cheltenham Spa](/wiki/Cheltenham_Spa "Cheltenham Spa") to recuperate and in August visited the [Bishop of Worcester](/wiki/Bishop_of_Worcester "Bishop of Worcester") at [Hartlebury Castle](/wiki/Hartlebury_Castle "Hartlebury Castle")[[98]](#cite_note-104) and [Viscount Mount Edgcumbe](/wiki/Earl_of_Mount_Edgcumbe "Earl of Mount Edgcumbe") at [Cotehele](/wiki/Cotehele "Cotehele"), Cornwall, with the Queen, and their daughters the Princess Royal and Princesses Augusta and Elizabeth.[[99]](#cite_note-105) It was the furthest he had ever been from London, but his condition worsened. In November of that year, he became seriously deranged, sometimes speaking for many hours without pause, causing him to foam at the mouth and his voice to become hoarse. George would frequently repeat himself and write sentences with over 400 words at a time, and his vocabulary became "more complex, creative and colourful", possible symptoms of bipolar disorder.[[100]](#cite_note-106) His doctors were largely at a loss to explain his illness, and spurious stories about his condition spread, such as the claim that he had shaken hands with a tree in the mistaken belief that it was the [King of Prussia](/wiki/Frederick_William_II_of_Prussia "Frederick William II of Prussia").[[101]](#cite_note-107) Treatment for mental illness was primitive by modern standards; George's doctors, who included [Francis Willis](/wiki/Francis_Willis_(physician) "Francis Willis (physician)"), treated the King by forcibly restraining him until he was calm, or applying caustic [poultices](/wiki/Poultice "Poultice") to draw out "evil humours".[[102]](#cite_note-108)

In the reconvened Parliament, Fox and Pitt wrangled over the terms of a regency during the King's incapacity. While both agreed that it would be most reasonable for the Prince of Wales to act as regent, Fox suggested, to Pitt's consternation, that it was the Prince's absolute right to act on his ill father's behalf with full powers. Pitt, fearing he would be removed from office if the Prince of Wales were so empowered, argued that it was for Parliament to nominate a regent, and wanted to restrict the regent's authority.[[103]](#cite_note-109) In February 1789, the [Regency Bill](/wiki/Regency_Acts#Regency_Bill_1789 "Regency Acts"), authorising the Prince of Wales to act as regent, was introduced and passed in the House of Commons, but before the House of Lords could pass the bill, George recovered.[[104]](#cite_note-110)

He would suffer brief temporary relapses in 1801 and 1804, followed by permanent illness from 1810 onward.[[105]](#cite_note-111)

## Later reign

### War in Europe

[![George wearing the red jacket of an 1800 British army general with the star of the Order of the Garter, white breeches, black knee-high boots, and a black bicorne hat. Behind him a groom holds a horse.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Portrait_of_King_George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom_%281738-1820%29%2C_by_Sir_William_Beechey_%281753-1839%29_-_Royal_Collection_%28RCIN_405422%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_King_George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom_%281738-1820%29%2C_by_Sir_William_Beechey_%281753-1839%29_-_Royal_Collection_%28RCIN_405422%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_King_George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom_(1738-1820),_by_Sir_William_Beechey_(1753-1839)_-_Royal_Collection_(RCIN_405422).jpg)

[Portrait of George III](/wiki/Portrait_of_George_III_(Beechey) "Portrait of George III (Beechey)") by Sir [William Beechey](/wiki/William_Beechey "William Beechey"), 1799/1800

After George's recovery, his popularity, and that of Pitt, continued to increase at the expense of Fox and the Prince of Wales.[[106]](#cite_note-112) His humane and understanding treatment of two insane assailants, [Margaret Nicholson](/wiki/Margaret_Nicholson "Margaret Nicholson") in 1786 and [John Frith](/wiki/John_Frith_(assailant) "John Frith (assailant)") in 1790, contributed to his popularity.[[107]](#cite_note-113) [James Hadfield](/wiki/James_Hadfield "James Hadfield")'s failed attempt to shoot George in the [Theatre Royal, Drury Lane](/wiki/Theatre_Royal,_Drury_Lane "Theatre Royal, Drury Lane"), on 15 May 1800 was not political in origin but motivated by the apocalyptic delusions of Hadfield and [Bannister Truelock](/wiki/Bannister_Truelock "Bannister Truelock"). George seemed unperturbed by the incident, so much so that he fell asleep in the interval.[[108]](#cite_note-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Henry_Marriott_Paget_-_Attack_on_the_royal_carriage%2C_1795.jpg/250px-Henry_Marriott_Paget_-_Attack_on_the_royal_carriage%2C_1795.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henry_Marriott_Paget_-_Attack_on_the_royal_carriage,_1795.jpg)

Attack on the royal carriage in 1795[[109]](#cite_note-115)

The [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") of 1789, in which the [French monarchy](/wiki/French_monarchy "French monarchy") had been overthrown, worried many British landowners. [France](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") declared war on Great Britain in 1793; in response to the crisis, George allowed Pitt to increase taxes, raise armies, and suspend the right of *[habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus")*. Pitt [prosecuted British radicals for treason](/wiki/1794_Treason_Trials "1794 Treason Trials") in 1794, and in October 1795, crowds attacked George's carriage on his way to opening Parliament, demanding an end to the war and lower bread prices. In response, Parliament passed the [Treason](/wiki/Treason_Act_1795 "Treason Act 1795") and [Seditious Meetings](/wiki/Seditious_Meetings_Act_1795 "Seditious Meetings Act 1795") Acts a month later.[[110]](#cite_note-116) The [First Coalition](/wiki/First_Coalition "First Coalition") to oppose revolutionary France, which included [Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"), and [Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700-1808) "History of Spain (1700-1808)"), broke up in 1795 when Prussia and Spain made separate peace with France.[[111]](#cite_note-117) The [Second Coalition](/wiki/Second_Coalition "Second Coalition"), which included Austria, [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), and the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), was defeated in 1800. Only Great Britain was left fighting [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte"), the [First Consul](/wiki/First_Consul "First Consul") of the French Republic.

A brief lull in hostilities allowed Pitt to concentrate effort on Ireland, where there had been [an uprising and attempted French landing in 1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798").[[112]](#cite_note-118) In 1800, the [British](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") and [Irish parliaments](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") passed an [Act of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800") that took effect on 1 January 1801 and united [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") and [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland") into a single state, known as the [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland"). George used the opportunity to abandon the title "King of France", which [English and British sovereigns had maintained](/wiki/British_claims_to_the_French_throne "British claims to the French throne") since the reign of [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III").[[113]](#cite_note-weir286-119) It was suggested that George adopt the title "[Emperor of the British Isles](/wiki/British_Emperor "British Emperor")", but he refused.[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12) As part of his Irish policy, Pitt planned to remove certain [legal disabilities](/wiki/Anti-Catholicism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Anti-Catholicism in the United Kingdom") that applied to [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Roman Catholicism in the United Kingdom"). George III claimed that to emancipate Catholics would be to violate his [coronation oath](/wiki/Coronation_oath_of_the_British_monarch "Coronation oath of the British monarch"), in which sovereigns promise to maintain Protestantism.[[114]](#cite_note-120) Faced with opposition to his religious reform policies from both the King and the British public, Pitt threatened to resign.[[115]](#cite_note-121) At about the same time, George had a relapse of his previous illness, which he blamed on worry over the Catholic question.[[116]](#cite_note-122) On 14 March 1801, Pitt was formally replaced by the [Speaker of the House of Commons](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_House_of_Commons_(United_Kingdom) "Speaker of the House of Commons (United Kingdom)"), [Henry Addington](/wiki/Henry_Addington "Henry Addington"). Addington opposed emancipation, instituted annual accounts, abolished income tax and began a programme of disarmament. In October 1801, he made peace with the French, and in 1802 signed the [Treaty of Amiens](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amiens "Treaty of Amiens").[[117]](#cite_note-123)

[![A span-high Napoleon stands on the outstretched hand of a full-size George III, who peers at him through a spy-glass.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/James_Gillray_The_King_of_Brobdingnag_and_Gulliver.%E2%80%93Vide._Swift%27s_Gulliver-_Voyage_to_Brobdingnag_The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_edit.jpg/250px-James_Gillray_The_King_of_Brobdingnag_and_Gulliver.%E2%80%93Vide._Swift%27s_Gulliver-_Voyage_to_Brobdingnag_The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Gillray_The_King_of_Brobdingnag_and_Gulliver.%E2%80%93Vide._Swift%27s_Gulliver-_Voyage_to_Brobdingnag_The_Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_edit.jpg)

Caricature by [James Gillray](/wiki/James_Gillray "James Gillray") of George holding Napoleon in the palm of his hand, 1803

George did not consider the peace with France as real; in his view it was an "experiment".[[118]](#cite_note-124) The war resumed in 1803, but public opinion distrusted Addington to lead the nation in war, and instead favoured Pitt. An [invasion of England by Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon%27s_planned_invasion_of_the_United_Kingdom "Napoleon's planned invasion of the United Kingdom") seemed imminent, and a massive volunteer movement arose to [defend England against the French](/wiki/British_anti-invasion_preparations_of_1803%E2%80%9305 "British anti-invasion preparations of 1803–05"). George's review of 27,000 volunteers in [Hyde Park, London](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London"), on 26 and 28 October 1803 and at the height of the invasion scare, attracted an estimated 500,000 spectators on each day.[[119]](#cite_note-125) *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* said: "The enthusiasm of the multitude was beyond all expression."[[120]](#cite_note-126) A courtier wrote on 13 November that "The King is really prepared to take the field in case of attack, his beds are ready and he can move at half an hour's warning."[[121]](#cite_note-127) George wrote to his friend [Bishop Hurd](/wiki/Richard_Hurd_(bishop) "Richard Hurd (bishop)"), "We are here in daily expectation that Bonaparte will attempt his threatened invasion ... Should his troops effect a landing, I shall certainly put myself at the head of mine, and my other armed subjects, to repel them."[[122]](#cite_note-128) After Admiral [Lord Nelson](/wiki/Lord_Nelson "Lord Nelson")'s famous naval victory at the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"), the possibility of invasion was extinguished.[[123]](#cite_note-129)

[![The King, his face obscured by a pillar, kicks out at the behinds of a group of well-fed ministers.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/A-Kick-at-the-Broad-Bottoms-Gillray.jpeg/500px-A-Kick-at-the-Broad-Bottoms-Gillray.jpeg)](/wiki/File:A-Kick-at-the-Broad-Bottoms-Gillray.jpeg)

In *A Kick at the [Broad-Bottoms](/wiki/Broad_Bottom_Government "Broad Bottom Government")!* (1807), James Gillray caricatured George's dismissal of the Ministry of All the Talents.

In 1804, George's recurrent illness returned; after his recovery, Addington resigned and Pitt regained power. Pitt sought to appoint Fox to [his ministry](/wiki/Second_Pitt_ministry "Second Pitt ministry"), but George refused. [Lord Grenville](/wiki/Lord_Grenville "Lord Grenville") perceived an injustice to Fox, and refused to join the new ministry.[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12) Pitt concentrated on forming a coalition with Austria, Russia, and [Sweden](/wiki/Gustavian_era "Gustavian era"). This [Third Coalition](/wiki/Third_Coalition "Third Coalition"), however, met the same fate as the First and Second Coalitions, collapsing in 1805. The setbacks in Europe took a toll on Pitt's health, and he died in 1806, reopening the question of who should serve in the ministry. Grenville became prime minister, and his "[Ministry of All the Talents](/wiki/Ministry_of_All_the_Talents "Ministry of All the Talents")" included Fox. Grenville pushed through the [Slave Trade Act 1807](/wiki/Slave_Trade_Act_1807 "Slave Trade Act 1807"), which passed both houses of Parliament with large majorities.[[124]](#cite_note-bitesize-130) George was conciliatory towards Fox, after being forced to capitulate over his appointment. After Fox's death in September 1806, the King and ministry were in open conflict. To boost recruitment, the ministry proposed a measure in February 1807 whereby Roman Catholics would be allowed to serve in all ranks of the armed forces. George instructed them not only to drop the measure, but also to agree never to set up such a measure again. The ministers agreed to drop the measure then pending, but refused to bind themselves in the future.[[125]](#cite_note-131) They were dismissed and replaced by the Duke of Portland, as the nominal prime minister, with actual power being held by the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer"), [Spencer Perceval](/wiki/Spencer_Perceval "Spencer Perceval"). Parliament was dissolved, and the [subsequent election](/wiki/1807_United_Kingdom_general_election "1807 United Kingdom general election") gave the ministry a strong majority in the House of Commons. George III made no further major political decisions during his reign; the replacement of Portland by Perceval in 1809 was of little real significance.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

### Final years and death

[![Monochrome profile of elderly George with a long white beard](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/George_III_by_Henry_Meyer.jpg/250px-George_III_by_Henry_Meyer.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_III_by_Henry_Meyer.jpg)

Engraving by [Henry Meyer](/wiki/Henry_Hoppner_Meyer "Henry Hoppner Meyer"), 1817, depicting an elderly George

In late 1810, at the height of his popularity,[[127]](#cite_note-133) King George, already virtually blind with [cataracts](/wiki/Cataract "Cataract") and in pain from [rheumatism](/wiki/Rheumatism "Rheumatism"), suffered a relapse into his mental disorder and became dangerously ill. In his view, the malady had been triggered by stress over the death of his youngest and favourite daughter, [Princess Amelia](/wiki/Princess_Amelia_of_the_United_Kingdom "Princess Amelia of the United Kingdom").[[128]](#cite_note-134) The princess's nurse reported that "the scenes of distress and crying every day ... were melancholy beyond description."[[129]](#cite_note-135) George accepted the need for the [Regency Act 1811](/wiki/Regency_Act_1811 "Regency Act 1811"),[[130]](#cite_note-136) and the Prince of Wales (later [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV")) acted as regent for the remainder of the King's life. Despite signs of a recovery in May 1811, by the end of the year, George III had become permanently insane, and lived in seclusion at Windsor Castle until his death.[[131]](#cite_note-137)

Prime Minister Spencer Perceval was [assassinated in 1812](/wiki/Assassination_of_Spencer_Perceval "Assassination of Spencer Perceval") and was replaced by [Lord Liverpool](/wiki/Lord_Liverpool "Lord Liverpool"). Liverpool oversaw British victory in the Napoleonic Wars. The subsequent [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") led to significant territorial gains for Hanover, which was elevated from an [electorate](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") to a [kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hanover "Kingdom of Hanover"). Meanwhile, George's health deteriorated. He developed [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), and became completely blind and increasingly deaf. He was incapable of knowing or understanding that he was declared King of Hanover in 1814, or that his wife died in 1818.[[132]](#cite_note-138) At Christmas 1819, he spoke nonsense for 58 hours, and for the last few weeks of his life was unable to walk.[[133]](#cite_note-139)

George died of [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia") at [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle") at 8:38 pm on 29 January 1820, aged 81, six days after the death of his fourth son [Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn").[[134]](#cite_note-Black,_p._410-140) His favourite son, [Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany](/wiki/Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany"), was with him.[[135]](#cite_note-141) He [lay in state](/wiki/Lay_in_state "Lay in state") for two days, and his funeral and interment took place on 16 February in [St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle](/wiki/St_George%27s_Chapel,_Windsor_Castle "St George's Chapel, Windsor Castle").[[134]](#cite_note-Black,_p._410-140)[[136]](#cite_note-142)[[137]](#cite_note-143)

## Slavery

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/DunmoresProclamation.jpg/250px-DunmoresProclamation.jpg)](/wiki/File:DunmoresProclamation.jpg)

[Dunmore's Proclamation](/wiki/Dunmore%27s_Proclamation "Dunmore's Proclamation"), by the King's authority, set free Rebel slaves.

Over the course of George's reign, a coalition of [abolitionists](/wiki/Abolitionists "Abolitionists") and [Atlantic slave](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") uprisings caused the British public to spurn slavery. According to the historian [Andrew Roberts](/wiki/Andrew_Roberts_(historian) "Andrew Roberts (historian)"), "George never bought or sold a slave in his life. He never invested in any of the companies that did such a thing. He signed legislation to abolish slavery." George wrote a document in the 1750s "denouncing all of the arguments for slavery, and calling them an execration and ridiculous and 'absurd'",[[138]](#cite_note-144) but the King and his son, the [Duke of Clarence](/wiki/William_IV "William IV"), supported the efforts of the [London Society of West India Planters and Merchants](/wiki/London_Society_of_West_India_Planters_and_Merchants "London Society of West India Planters and Merchants") to delay the abolition of the British slave trade for almost 20 years.[[139]](#cite_note-newman-145)[[140]](#cite_note-146)

On 7 November 1775, during the American War of Independence, [John Murray, 4th Earl of Dunmore](/wiki/John_Murray,_4th_Earl_of_Dunmore "John Murray, 4th Earl of Dunmore"), issued a proclamation that offered freedom to the slaves of rebel masters if they enlisted to put down the colonial rebellion. Dunmore was the last [Royal Governor of Virginia](/wiki/Royal_Governor_of_Virginia "Royal Governor of Virginia"), appointed by King George III in July 1771. [Dunmore's Proclamation](/wiki/Dunmore%27s_Proclamation "Dunmore's Proclamation") inspired slaves to escape from captivity and fight for the British. On 30 June 1779, George III's Commanding General [Henry Clinton](/wiki/Henry_Clinton_(British_Army_officer,_born_1730) "Henry Clinton (British Army officer, born 1730)") broadened Dunmore's proclamation with his [Philipsburg Proclamation](/wiki/Philipsburg_Proclamation "Philipsburg Proclamation"). For all colonial slaves who fled their rebel masters, Clinton forbade their recapture and resale, giving them protection by the British military. Approximately 20,000 freed slaves joined the British, fighting for George III. In 1783, given British certificates of freedom, 3,000 former slaves, including their families, settled in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia").[[141]](#cite_note-147)

Between 1791 and 1800, almost 400,000 Africans were shipped to the Americas by 1,340 slaving voyages mounted from British ports. On 25 March 1807, George III signed into law *[An Act for the Abolition of the Slave Trade](/wiki/An_Act_for_the_Abolition_of_the_Slave_Trade "An Act for the Abolition of the Slave Trade")*, under which the transatlantic slave trade was banned in the British Empire.[[142]](#cite_note-148)

## Legacy

George was succeeded by two of his sons, [George IV](/wiki/George_IV "George IV") and [William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV") in turn, who both died without surviving legitimate children, leaving the throne to [Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"), the only legitimate child of his fourth son Prince Edward.

George III lived for 81 years and 239 days, and reigned for 59 years and 96 days: both his life and his reign were longer than those of any of his predecessors and subsequent kings; only queens Victoria and [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") [lived](/wiki/List_of_longest-living_members_of_the_British_royal_family "List of longest-living members of the British royal family") and [reigned longer](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_in_Britain_by_length_of_reign "List of monarchs in Britain by length of reign").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/TransitOfVenus1769.png/250px-TransitOfVenus1769.png)](/wiki/File:TransitOfVenus1769.png)

Extract from *Observations on the Transit of Venus*, a manuscript notebook from the collections of George III, showing George, Charlotte and those attending them.

George III was dubbed "Farmer George" by satirists, at first to mock his interest in mundane matters rather than politics, but later to portray him as a man of the people, contrasting his homely thrift with his son's grandiosity.[[143]](#cite_note-149) Under George III, the [British Agricultural Revolution](/wiki/British_Agricultural_Revolution "British Agricultural Revolution") reached its peak and great advances were made in fields such as science and industry. There was unprecedented growth in the rural population, which in turn provided much of the workforce for the concurrent [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution").[[144]](#cite_note-150) George's collection of mathematical and scientific instruments is now owned by [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London") but housed in the [Science Museum, London](/wiki/Science_Museum,_London "Science Museum, London"), to which it has been on long-term loan since 1927. He had the [King's Observatory](/wiki/King%27s_Observatory "King's Observatory") built in [Richmond-upon-Thames](/wiki/Richmond-upon-Thames "Richmond-upon-Thames") for his own observations of the [1769 transit of Venus](/wiki/1769_transit_of_Venus "1769 transit of Venus"). When [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") discovered [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus") in 1781, he at first named it *Georgium Sidus* (George's Star) after the King, who later funded the construction and maintenance of Herschel's 1785 [40-foot telescope](/wiki/40-foot_telescope "40-foot telescope"), which at the time was the biggest ever built.

George III hoped that "the tongue of malice may not paint my intentions in those colours she admires, nor the sycophant extoll me beyond what I deserve"[[145]](#cite_note-151) but, in the popular mind, George III has been both demonised and praised. While very popular at the start of his reign, by the mid-1770s George had lost the loyalty of revolutionary American colonists.[[146]](#cite_note-152) The grievances in the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") were presented as "repeated injuries and usurpations" that he had committed to establish an "absolute Tyranny" over the colonies. The declaration's wording has contributed to the American public's perception of George as a tyrant. Contemporary accounts of George III's life fall into two camps: one demonstrating "attitudes dominant in the latter part of the reign, when the King had become a revered symbol of national resistance to French ideas and French power", while the other "derived their views of the King from the bitter partisan strife of the first two decades of the reign, and they expressed in their works the views of the opposition".[[147]](#cite_note-153)

Building on the latter of these two assessments, British historians of the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, such as [Trevelyan](/wiki/Sir_George_Trevelyan,_2nd_Baronet "Sir George Trevelyan, 2nd Baronet") and [Erskine May](/wiki/Erskine_May "Erskine May"), promoted hostile interpretations of George III's life. However, in the mid-twentieth century the work of [Lewis Namier](/wiki/Lewis_Namier "Lewis Namier"), who thought George was "much maligned", started a re-evaluation of the man and his reign.[[148]](#cite_note-154) Scholars of the later twentieth century, such as [Butterfield](/wiki/Herbert_Butterfield "Herbert Butterfield") and Pares, and Macalpine and Hunter,[[149]](#cite_note-155) are inclined to treat George sympathetically, seeing him as a victim of circumstance and illness. Butterfield rejected the arguments of his Victorian predecessors with withering disdain: "Erskine May must be a good example of the way in which an historian may fall into error through an excess of brilliance. His capacity for synthesis, and his ability to dovetail the various parts of the evidence ... carried him into a more profound and complicated elaboration of error than some of his more pedestrian predecessors ... he inserted a doctrinal element into his history which, granted his original aberrations, was calculated to project the lines of his error, carrying his work still further from centrality or truth."[[150]](#cite_note-156) In pursuing war with the American colonists, George III believed he was defending the right of an elected Parliament to levy taxes, rather than seeking to expand his own power or prerogatives.[[151]](#cite_note-157) In the opinion of modern scholars, during the long reign of George III, the monarchy continued to lose its political power and grew as the embodiment of national morality.[[9]](#cite_note-dnb-12)

## Titles, styles, honours and arms

### Titles and styles

* 4 June 1738 – 31 March 1751: *His Royal Highness* Prince George[[152]](#cite_note-158)
* 31 March 1751 – 20 April 1751: *His Royal Highness* The Duke of Edinburgh
* 20 April 1751 – 25 October 1760: *His Royal Highness* The Prince of Wales
* 25 October 1760 – 29 January 1820: *His Majesty* The King

In Great Britain, George III used the official [style](/wiki/Style_(manner_of_address) "Style (manner of address)") "George the Third, by the Grace of God, King of Great Britain, France, and Ireland, [Defender of the Faith](/wiki/Defender_of_the_Faith "Defender of the Faith"), and so forth". In 1801, when [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") united with [Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"), he dropped the title of King of France, which had been used for every English monarch since [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III "Edward III")'s [claim to the French throne](/wiki/British_claims_to_the_French_throne "British claims to the French throne") in the medieval period.[[113]](#cite_note-weir286-119) His style became "George the Third, by the Grace of God, of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland King, Defender of the Faith."[[153]](#cite_note-p390-159)

In Germany, he was "Duke of [Brunswick and Lüneburg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brunswick-L%C3%BCneburg "Duchy of Brunswick-Lüneburg"), [Arch-Treasurer](/wiki/Arch-Treasurer "Arch-Treasurer") and [Prince-elector](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire")" (*Herzog von Braunschweig und Lüneburg, Erzschatzmeister und Kurfürst des Heiligen Römischen Reiches*[[154]](#cite_note-160)) until the end of the empire in 1806. He then continued as duke until the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") declared him "King of Hanover" in 1814.[[153]](#cite_note-p390-159)
